data.title: Genmab's Outlook Beyond Darzalex's Patent Cliff
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Solomon Oshabaheebwa as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Genmab (GMAB) presents a potentially rewarding long-term investment opportunity, though current uncertainties warrant a cautious approach. The primary concern is the pending patent expiration for Darzalex, Genmab's blockbuster drug that contributed 65% of FY24 revenue. In this analysis, I assessed how Genmab is positioned to weather this significant transition. I examined the revenue potential of Genmab’s other commercial products, its late-stage pipeline, anticipated product launches, and balance sheet strength. I then made revenue projections that model "best-case" and "worst-case" scenarios as Darzalex royalties decline. Despite the looming patent cliff, my findings suggest that Genmab's robust pipeline and strategic positioning make it a compelling low-risk, high-reward opportunity for patient investors.

Company Overview

Genmab develops customized antibodies that target markers on the surface of cancerous cells or other molecules associated with various illnesses. They make the antibodies through five proprietary technology platforms termed: DuoBody, HexaBody, DuoHexaBody, HexElect, and Antibody-Drug Conjugates (ADCs). They have successfully licensed their technology to several Biotech behemoths, including Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Novartis (NVS), and Amgen (AMGN), that incorporate the antibodies in various therapies. These partnerships yield milestone revenues tied to certain development goals, and royalties on sales.

Until recently, Genmab did not participate in commercialization instead focusing on technology development. It began to expand its own commercialization capabilities with TIVDAK for cervical cancer in 2021, sharing commercial responsibilities and profits with Pfizer in various markets. Starting 2025, Genmab will have sole responsibility outside the US and China. The company also co-commercializes EPKINLY/TEPKINLY with AbbVie, leading efforts in the US and Japan while AbbVie takes charge in Europe. The company aims to expand its wholly owned portfolio in the near term with new candidates Rina-S and Acasunlimab.

DARZALEX’s dominance presents a concentration risk.

In FY2024, Genmab reported revenues of $3 billion of which 65% was generated from DARZALEX royalties. This heavy dependence on a single product poses a significant vulnerability to future revenues as patent expiration approaches. As I will discuss later, the other products on the market are unlikely to outgrow Darzalex sales in the near term.

J&J collaboration on DARZALEX is expiring soon.

J&J and Genmab have an existing global commercialization agreement in which Genmab granted J&J an exclusive license to develop and sell products containing daratumumab (the active ingredient in DARZALEX) for the treatment of patients with certain indications of multiple myeloma [MM] and light-chain [AL] amyloidosis. In exchange, Genmab receives up to 20% royalties from the net sales of these products paid until the expiration of the last relevant Genmab patents. These patents, after accounting for extensions, begin to expire in 2029 (U.S.), 2030 (Japan) and 2031 (Europe). Because of the nature of the agreement, DARZALEX royalties are projected to decline to near 0 by 2032, the only question is whether Genmab can open up new revenue streams to make up for this loss.

HexaBody-CD38 was a failed succession plan.

Patent expiration is a common risk in the Biotech sector with the potential to dramatically erode a company's revenue and market value. Pharmaceutical companies are typically granted 20-year patents from the point of discovery, which includes research and development time. This effectively leaves most drugs with approximately 10 years of market exclusivity after which competitors can introduce generic versions, often at substantially lower prices, resulting in a steep decline in sales (the "patent cliff"). Pharmaceutical companies often navigate this cliff by developing improved versions of their existing drugs, and proactively transitioning patients to the new formulations before the original patent expires, thereby maintaining their competitive edge and market position.

HexaBody-CD38 (GEN3014) was GenMab’s strategy to replace DARZALEX and maintain revenue streams from the same patient population. HexaBody-CD38 targets the same antibody CD38 as DARZALEX but is designed to be more potent. It uses Genmab’s HexaBody technology to form clusters upon target binding that make it better at mobilizing the complementary immune system to kill myeloma cells with higher potency. In head-to-head clinical trials against DARZALEX, Hexabody-CD38 showed promising results with a better overall response rate (55% versus 52%). But J&J did not find the results sufficient to warrant further investment. The decision made sense as the new drug must be much better than the old drug to convince patients and prescribers to abandon the old drug especially when the old drug gets cheaper with generic versions in the market. While the performance of HexaBody-CD38 demonstrated potential for Genmab’s HexaBody technology in future applications, it failed to succeed as a replacement for DARZALEX and left Genmab still exposed to the patent risk.

Can Existing Products Fill the DARZALEX Gap?

Genmab provided future sales projections for most of its commercialized products. These projections are largely in line with those made by other independent analysts but keep in mind that it shares revenue with collaborators on all its products. DARZALEX, Kesimpta, and TEPEZZA are expected to grow moderately at a high-single-digit compound annual growth rate [CAGR] to about $16.4 billion in 2030. TECVAYLI, TALVEY, RYBREVANT, have a much higher runway with projections assuming double-digit CAGRs but Genmab’s royalties on these products will remain much smaller than DARZALEX.

In TIVDAK (cervical cancer), starting in 2025, Pfizer will lead commercialization activities in the US and China with a 50:50 profit split, while Genmab will lead commercialization elsewhere and pay Pfizer royalties in the low teens. TIVDAK annual sales were $131M in 2024 and are projected to reach $863M in 2030.

For EPKINLY/TEPKINLY (large B-cell lymphomas), Genmab and AbbVie share profits 50:50 in the U.S. and Japan, while Genmab receives 22% to 26% royalties from other regions. Global sales of EPKINLY/TEPKINLY were $281M in 2024 and are expected to grow to $1.9B by 2029 with additional growth contingent on the outcomes of on-going phase 3 clinical trials.

Overall, Genmab has a large product portfolio with high expectations for sales growth. Even if these targets are met, DARZALEX royalties remain unmatched and the concentration risk will persist as the blockbuster drug will still account for around 70% of annual royalty revenue in 2030. Genmab's best hope is launching new products from its clinical pipeline to generate new revenue streams.

Diversified late-stage pipeline with potential product launches.

Genmab has 7 ongoing phase 3 trials, including 5 for EPKINLY (in collaboration with AbbVie), aiming to expand the patient population and indications in B-cell malignancies. Other programs involve wholly owned new therapies - Rina-S for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer, and Acasunlimab for solid tumors.

While clinical trials inherently possess a high risk of failure (some estimates suggest 50% – 60% failure rate), the risk is significantly lower for clinical trials expanding the use of an already approved drug primarily because the safety profile and pharmacokinetics of the drug are already established. I am therefore optimistic about the expected new product launches, especially for the expansion of EPKINLY, which is already FDA-approved.

The results of these clinical trials, if positive, will lead to new product launches between 2027 and 2031 with potential peak sales above $3 billion for EPKINLY, $2 billion from Rina S, and $1 billion from Acasunlimab. These products will certainly have a major impact on Genmab’s value and investors have good reason to keep an eye out for updates from the clinical studies to gain clarity on the probability of success.

Strength of Balance sheet

Genmab ended FY 2024 with a strong balance sheet with $2.9 billion in cash and investments and no interest-bearing long-term debt. Historically, Genmab converts about 35% of revenue to free cash flow, assuming this trend continues in the near term, the company will generate over $1 billion per year in free cash (even at the lower end of expected revenues as discussed in the next section). This cash position indicates that they can fully fund their clinical pipeline while maintaining substantial reserves for funding strategic acquisitions similar to the $1.8 billion acquisition of ProfoundBio, or return value to shareholders through share repurchases as they’ve done in the past.

Future revenue projections

The major risk for Genmab is Scientific Risk. I modeled two scenarios to assess its impact on Genmab's future revenue. In both scenarios, I assumed that DARZALEX sales will grow at a 7% CAGR until 2029, and then decline to $0 by 2032. Meanwhile, other commercialized products grow at 23% CAGR over the next five years, followed by a 10% growth rate thereafter.

In the first scenario, I assumed that all phase 3 trials will fail. Based on these assumptions, my model predicts that Genmab's revenue could reach its peak in 2029 and then bottom out at approximately $2.9 billion in 2032.

In the second scenario, I assumed that all phase 3 trials will succeed, with new products launching as expected from 2027 to 2031 and steadily growing to reach expected maturity revenues within 5 years after launch. In this scenario, the model predicts rapidly growing revenues between 2027 – 2029 as new products launch and then moderate growth thereafter as new product revenue offsets the loss of DARZALEX royalties, reaching ~$7.5 billion by 2033.

Valuation

The biotech sector has had low investor confidence since 2022 with several Biotech companies now trading at historically low sales multiples including Genmab’s current 3.9x. If we assume that this sales multiple remains flat, Genmab could be trading at a market cap of $12.5 billion in scenario 1 and $30 billion in scenario 2. Thus in a worst-case scenario, the price remains roughly flat, while in a best-case scenario, the price goes up nearly 3x the current value.

Risks

There are some risks worthy of note. Rina S will directly compete with Elahere (mirvetuximab soravtansine-gynx) by Immunogen (owned by ABBV), which is also an ADC targeting platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. Elahare received FDA approval in 2024 giving it a head start in this space. EPKINLY also faces competition from several CAR-T therapies and other antibody therapies that are already approved for the same indications in both B-cell malignancies and follicular lymphoma. The competition from these alternative therapies could significantly decrease market penetration for Genmab’s candidates resulting in underwhelming sales.

Additionally, the company intends to make more acquisitions which could increase the debt burden and lower future cash inflows. Revenues from the other commercialized products could also decelerate under pressure from competitors.

Conclusion

On balance, I believe Genmab’s current market cap of ~$11.8 billion roughly aligns with a scenario where all its phase-3 trials fail. The company has a Quant score of 3.37 and a “HOLD” rating which is highly leveraged by an “F” growth factor grade while the other factors remain strong. It is likely to receive an upgrade if the growth problem is solved with the introduction of new products with substantial contributions to revenue and high growth prospects. If the ongoing phase 3 trials are successful, Genmab could see a 3x appreciation in value. Also, consider that the company can utilize its strong cash position to open new avenues for growth such as new acquisitions or advancing other early-stage clinical programs.

At the current valuation, the downside is limited while the potential upside is substantial making it potentially a low-risk high-reward investment. The only reason I didn’t give it a buy rating is because I believe it will take some time for this value to materialize as major results from clinical trials are not expected until 2026. Investors should watch the company closely, particularly for updates from the clinical studies. If these studies prove successful, a price bump could be expected.

data.title: Estee Lauder: Do Not Expect Beauty Reimagined To Turnaround The Company In The Short-Term
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Gunn Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

Estee Lauder (NYSE:EL) is one of the largest cosmetic companies in the world that is headquarted in New York, United States and employs about 62k full-time employees. The company's products include skin care, fragrance, makeup, and hair care; these products are sold in more than 150 countries under multiple renowned brands such as Estee Lauder, Jo Malone, Clinique, Aramis, and more.

Based on my analysis, although EL's share price has declined about 85% from its 2022 high, it is not a buying opportunity. While the company launched Beauty Reimagined, macroeconomic factors such as deteriorating consumer sentiment and the current trade war will continue to weigh on the performance of EL. In this report, I will demonstrate why investors should exercise caution and avoid EL in their portfolios.

Latest Developments

For 2Q25, EL generated $4 billion in revenues, representing a y/y decline of 6.45%. Revenue deterioration is primarily due to weaker sales across all product categories and region. Specifically, revenue from skin care products declined by 12% due to a more challenging retail environment in Asia Pacific while revenue from hair care products fell by 8% due to a continued lack of demand in the company's salon channel. From a geographical perspective, sales in EMEA and Asia Pacific saw the largest deterioration with a revenue decline of 6% and 11% respectively.

Operating margin for the period has deteriorated to 11.54% from 13.60% in the same period last year due to higher SG&A. Net income continues to remain in the negative territory; In 2Q25, net income margin deteriorated to -14.74% from 7.31% in the same period last year.

Looking ahead, EL anticipates financial deterioration to persist. Overall, organic sales is expected to decline by 8% to 10%. Furthermore, currency translation is likely to weigh on net sales by about 2%. Additionally, due to a change in geographical mix in earnings, effective tax will surge to 36% from 30.50%.

Challenging Consumer Environment Will Likely Exacerbate EL's Financial Deterioration

Currently, we are seeing multiple indicators that suggests that consumer sentiment across the world will remain weak. Based on Upside's consumer spending report, 58% of consumers will reduce spending due to a poor perception of the economy. Moreover, in a special survey conducted by Conference Board, consumers are still extremely price sensitive.

Thus far, EL's revenue deterioration is primarily due to a weak consumer sentiment in Asia Pacific and EMEA; the Americas region has largely outperformed. However, latest macroeconomic data suggests that EL will start to see further sales deterioration in the Americas region. Based on the latest data from the University of Michigan's US consumer survey, consumer sentiment has plunged 34.2% y/y to 50.8 in April 2025 from 77.2 in the same period last year; the latest survey highlighted that the risk of recession has increased and expectations for "business conditions, personal finances, incomes, inflation, and labor markets" have weakened substantially.

Tariff Tsunami and Reciprocal Tariffs will Weigh on Beauty Reimagined and Expanded Profit Recovery and Growth Plan

In an effort to combat the company's financial deterioration, EL has introduced and launched Beauty Reimagined to restore revenue growth and achieve double-digit operating margin within the next few years. Through Beauty Reimagined, EL will: (1) rapidly expand its portfolio presence, (2) boost consumer-facing investments such as increasing advertising spending, (3) expand its profit recovery and growth plan, and (4) optimize its operations by re-evaluating key supplier relationships and improving its supply chain network.

It is important to note that EL is engaged in manufacturing and distribution on the global scale. Even though EL may attempt to optimize its operations through workforce reductions, there is a strong likelihood that these efforts will be offset directly or indirectly by President Trump's tariff tsunami and reciprocal tariffs by other countries. Global companies like EL may attempt to localize its manufacturing and distribution; however, it will take some time before these companies can properly insulate themselves.

Moreover, the current trade war will exacerbate and accelerate the shift in consumer behaviors that we are seeing in recent years. Due to the rise in nationalism, Chinese consumers are already leaning more closely to local brands over international brands. Moreover, EL is already facing deteriorating sales in Asia Pacific; if the trade war persists, there is a strong likelihood that the company's sales will continue to weaken; in such a case, no amount of cost-saving initiatives will help the company.

Investors Should Expect Further Decline in Cash Reserves and Dividends Reduction In the Near Future

As Beauty Reimagined only recently launched, and we are still in the early stages of a global trade war, it is unlikely that we will see EL recovering in the near future. Hence, there is a likelihood that EL will burn through its cash reserves as it tries to pivot and navigate through the current challenges. Currently, EL only has $2.58 billion in cash and cash equivalents. Moreover, in the past twelve months, EL has net change in cash of -$1.35 billion; if this keeps up, there is a strong likelihood that EL will need to tap on the debt market to strengthen its liquidity.

As such, investors should not expect the current dividend distribution rate to be maintained. There is a strong possibility that EL will continue to reduce its dividends in an effort to preserve liquidity. In fact, in the past two quarters, EL already reduced its dividends by about 46% from $0.66 per share to $0.35 per share.

EL's 80% Drawdown From Its 2022 High Does Not Make the Company's Valuation Attractive

Ostensibly, it may appear that it is a good time to accumulate EL as the company's share price has declined about 85% from its 2022 high. However, from a relative valuation point of view, the company is still considered expensive. On a forward P/E non-GAAP basis, EL is trading at 39.46x as compared to its sector median of 15.72x. Even if we account for the company's debt, the company is trading at a forward EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA of 1.84x and 14.16x as compared to the sector median of 1.56x and 9.92x.

Hence, I would like to emphasize and reiterate, investors should exercise caution at this stage and should not be fooled into catching a falling knife. There are better avenues and opportunities out there in the market where investors can put their money to work.

Closing Remarks

Overall, although EL launched Beauty Reimagined as an effort to turnaround the company, investors should expect continued underperformance in the short term. The current global trade environment coupled with shifts in consumer behaviors and expectations will continue to weigh on the performance of EL. Moreover, the company's current valuation is not a screaming buy; investors will do better if they continue to exercise patience and put their money to work elsewhere.

EL is set to release its 3Q25 results on 1st May 2025. Previously, as many of the current tariffs were not yet imposed, the implications on EL were not discussed in the earnings call. Hence, in the upcoming earnings call, there is a strong possibility that management and analysts will center their discussion around the implications of the tariffs. Investors should pay close attention to how EL's management intend to navigate through the upcoming uncertainty.

data.title: Amazon: What ROIC Of AWS Says About The Stock's Perceived Fair Value
data.description:
Amazon, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) has been hit as hard as any of its peers in the famed Magnificent Seven (Mag 7) group of elite tech, retail, and communications stocks that account for nearly thirty percent of the S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

And yet, AMZN remains among the most attractive of these mega-caps, and the recent YTD decline of nearly 16 percent has only made it even more compelling. Not quite at fair value just yet, as I hope to show, but we seem to be getting there.

At a price to forward sales ratio close to its 5Y average, AMZN looks like it is at or very close to fair value. I say this in spite of it P/S multiple being well above the sector median because, for all practical purposes, AWS is still the one segment supporting earnings. Implicit in that is the need to look at this as an investment in cloud, not in retail and not yet in advertising.

I do not claim that other segments are not profitable, but my purpose with this article is to show the extent of AWS's ability to offset any kind of weakness in its retail and other operations, and act as a support to Amazon's stock price.

Allow me to begin with a quick look at a few key metrics that will set the base for my thesis.

How AWS Changed the Fortunes of Amazon.com, Inc.

I believe it would be more than a little pedantic to go through AWS' entire history with this audience, so we shall restrict our analysis to the past five years. My intention, as I alluded to earlier, is to showcase how this business segment, which Jeff Bezos called "A radical idea" in the FY14 Annual Report, is now the mainstay of Amazon's massive ship.

Driving Return on Capital

In that same Annual Report, Bezos made a statement that proved prophetic:

I’m optimistic that AWS will have strong returns on capital. This is one we as a team examine because AWS is capital intensive. The good news is we like what we see when we do these analyses. Structurally, AWS is far less capital intensive than the mode it’s replacing – do-it-yourself datacenters – which have low utilization rates, almost always below 20%. Pooling of workloads across customers gives AWS much higher utilization rates, and correspondingly higher capital efficiency. Further, once again our leadership position helps: scale economies can provide us a relative advantage on capital efficiency. We’ll continue to watch and shape the business for good returns on capital.

A maddeningly simple concept that has now grown to $107.6 billion in sales as of FY24, but what about the "strong returns on capital" that Bezos predicted?

We shall restrict ourselves to invested capital so we build a better picture of returns on active investments. When seen through that lens, it becomes self-evident that this core profitability metric has a high correlation to AMZN share price growth. When ROIC began dropping in FY22, share price followed. Of course, ROIC is tied to net profitability, so when net income turned negative that year, the annual return on invested capital turned negative as well.

It took an entire year for the net income and ROIC to recover, forcing the stock to stay below the $150 mark through almost the entire period. Our interest here is in AWS' contribution to the bottom line during that two-year window. To that end, I present the segmental annual results for FY22.

AWS had contributed 16 percent to overall revenue. Not exceptional in and of itself, but the $81 billion that it brought in that year accounted for ALL operating profitability. All $12.3 billion of it, and completely offsetting operating losses in the two retail segments.

When that positive EBIT hit the bottom line, it was negative, primarily due to other operating expenses. Net income came in at -$2.7 billion. Net invested capital using the operating approach (net working capital + net PP&E + acquired intangibles + goodwill + other) works out to a little over $240 billion. Further validating this with the financing approach to calculating invested capital (total debt + deferred taxes + other liabilities + preferred stock + common equity), we get a figure of $234 billion. Averaging those to $237 billion, we get an ROIC of approximately -1.14 percent.

My claim is that without AWS, Amazon's ROIC would have been significantly deeper in the red. In that regard, it acted as a floor for AMZN's stock price.

Advancing this thesis to FY24, it is once again self-evident that AWS has contributed the bulk of Amazon's profitability. While contributing only 17 percent to the top line, AWS reported an EBIT of $39.8 billion, contributing nearly 60 percent to the consolidated EBIT figure of $68.59 billion.

What to Expect in FY25

Now for the prickly subject of tariffs. CEO Jassy has recently indicated that Amazon's retail customers are likely to see price increases from sellers who "will pass that cost on". His reassurance that the company is "doing everything we can to try and keep prices the way they’ve been for customers — as low as possible." could mean that the company will bear some of the additional cost so the price jumps for customers are kept within bounds. Furthermore, demand is likely to drop for items that are subject to the highest tariff slabs, which means there will likely be more than a little weakness at the bottom line.

This is precisely where AWS and its contribution to ROIC will keep the stock trading at levels that would otherwise be unsustainable through FY25. I would remind investors that AWS is not completely immune to tariffs just because it offers digital services; the hit will come from higher technology costs related to the hardware it acquires to keep growing its operations.

One opportunity for AMZN is to put a tight cap on any capital expenditures not related to either maintenance or its retail business. By restricting invested capital in this fashion, the contribution to ROIC from AWS will be even more pronounced. This is one way to ensure a high ROIC in what I foresee to be a tough year for the company. The CEO's assurances should be taken with a pinch of salt, as they say, because the retail business has high exposure to China and other nations that the tariffs are targeting.

Whatever hit we see to Amazon's retail topline at home and overseas could be partially ameliorated by front-loading inventory, or as Jassy referred to it, "strategic forward inventory buys", which means we may not see its effect until at least 3Q25 or later. When it does hit, I suspect the impact will not be minimal. This eventuality, fortunately, will see AWS come to the rescue with its strong bottom-line profitability. As long as AMZN keeps its non-AWS capex to a bare minimum, the stock should be able to weather up to $40 billion or more in operating losses in the retail segment and still show positive ROIC. Assuming AWS revenues keep growing over FY25, that buffer could get bigger over time.

What Are My Risk Considerations?

My recommendation, as I initiate coverage on AMZN, is a Hold. I do have faith that AWS will play a major role in offering support for the stock through FY25, but I am equally aware of the risks. The most likely one is an inordinate and sustained dip in retail revenue growth.

Now that one sees clearly that revenue growth could take a heavy hit from tariffs, one should look at AWS' ROIC as the primary indicator of intrinsic value. This is where support for the stock will come from, as I see it. We shall attempt to swoop in on this sweet spot from an ROIC perspective.

A 20 percent return on invested capital in the last full-fiscal against a five-year average of 14 percent should have allowed the stock to move up further from where it was at the start of the year. Notwithstanding the general market disruption that began shortly after Trump took office, there is also another loose end, which is that the stock price has never been rewarded to this degree even when ROIC was approaching 24 percent in FY21. $175 seems to be the sweet spot for this stock even at those ROIC levels.

A closer look at the price chart above (purple line) suggests that any breach of the $175 level results in a rapid pullback to below that level. The YTD decline has yet to bring it down again, but I think it likely will.

On the whole, if this has indeed been the result of the market being unwilling to keep the stock above $175 despite record-breaking ROIC, then a further haircut on that $175 would be well-justified in this time of even greater uncertainty at the global level.

I shall not attempt to quantify that downside from $175 because the hit to the retail segment is unknowable at this point. We should be getting some fresh updates when 1Q25 results are out later this month, but the retail segment's 40 percent contribution to ROIC should last until 2Q25, by my estimation. Depending on how much of an inventory build up we see across 1Q and 2Q (the "strategic forward inventory buys" that Jassy referred to), we should be able to gauge the runway for further retail growth and assess how long it might be until the full effect of tariffs is brought to bear on Amazon's retail operations.

Until I have that visibility, I cannot recommend anything beyond a Hold. At the current price of $185, I think the stock is slightly overpriced compared to where it traded at peak ROIC - around $175. The stock's fortunes are now tied in squarely with the ROIC of AWS, so the only way I see the price going up from here is if Amazon's management is willing to make those capital investment sacrifices in its retail business and favor more efficient spending on maintaining and growing AWS infrastructure and offerings.

One particular point that the CEO highlighted on the 4Q24 call gives me confidence that these capex adjustments are, in fact, being made (bolded for emphasis by author):

Chips are the key ingredient in the compute that drives training and inference. Most AI compute has been driven by NVIDIA chips and we obviously have a deep partnership with NVIDIA and will for as long as we can see into the future.

However, there aren't that many generative AI applications at large scale yet. And when you get there as we have with apps like Alexa and Rufus, cost can get steep quickly. Customers want better price performance and it's why we built our own custom AI silicon.

By bringing down retail operating costs, AMZN can filter more dollars down to the bottom line. Combined with higher but more return-friendly capital spending on the AWS side of things, it should make for a very compelling ROIC profile despite the headwind risks I have discussed.

As I take leave of you, I would advise acquiring shares below $175 and then using a dollar cost averaging method to either optimize your cost basis if the stock goes up or reduce it if the stock declines further. I do not presume to know what the market is thinking right now, but the word 'overvaluation' is very likely buzzing around every investor's mind. I urge you to play it safe and leave yourself with an adequate margin of safety down from the market's perceived ROIC-based fair value of $175. Hold for now.

data.title: Trump Weighs New Tariffs And Auto Sector Relief
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Uncertainty lingers

Market participants remain wary of President Donald Trump's trade policies, with latest updates indicating more tariffs - and rollbacks - may be on the way. Pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports are under scrutiny for potential tariffs, while the automobile sector may be offered some respite in the days ahead.

Review underway: On April 1, the Trump administration began investigating the impact of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports on national security, likely setting the stage for tariffs on the sectors. The Department of Commerce's pharma investigation covers generic and non-generic drugs, active pharma ingredients, and derivative products. The semiconductor probe covers items including leading-edge chips, chipmaking equipment, silicon wafers, and "downstream products that contain semiconductors."

The probes will look into current and projected demand for the pharma and semiconductor products, the ability of domestic production to meet demand, and potential for export restrictions. The chip probe will also assess "the ability of foreign nations to weaponize their control over semiconductors and SME supply chains." The investigations were initiated under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which allows the president to adjust imports that are found to threaten national security.

Tariff relief? In other news, Trump floated a potential reprieve for the 25% tariffs on automobile and auto part imports, given the industry's deeply-integrated North American supply chains. "I'm looking at something to help some car companies with it," Trump told reporters. "They're switching to parts that were made in Canada, Mexico and other places… they need a little bit of time, because they're going to make them here." Trump's signal was enough to lift top automakers' shares - GM, F, STLA - which ended on a higher note on Monday.

What else is happening...

WSB survey results: Better trade deals for the U.S. are expected.

Yellen: Bond selloff signals 'loss of confidence' in policymaking.

Consumer polls - Mixed inflation outlook, confidence worsens.

Trump administration to end rule capping credit card late fee.

Lawmakers call on White House to get rid of AI Diffusion rule.

Palantir-NATO deal places it in 'prime position' for AI in defense.

Bristol Myers' (BMY) heart disease drug fails in late-stage trial.

Wall Street-backed crypto bank Anchorage under investigation.

Air travel slumps: U.S. airports see noticeable decline in traffic.

Over $2B of Harvard University's federal funding gets blocked.

Today's Markets
