data.title: PepsiCo's Valuation Hasn't Been This Low In A Decade - Time To Buy
data.description:
If the stock market doesn't like one thing, it's uncertainty.

The US-induced trade tensions, with back-and-forth announcements on the shape or size of import tariffs, have wreaked havoc on the market, with companies unable to make long-term CAPEX commitments as the rules of the game change daily.

Without clarity, it's particularly difficult for stock-pickers to be opportunistic, even in falling markets. At the moment, stocks are driven not by fundamentals but rather by politics.

In times like these, I prefer to flee to safety. I buy consumer staples such as my favorite, PepsiCo, Inc. (NASDAQ:PEP), reducing the risk in my portfolio while collecting a 3.75% dividend.

I have owned PepsiCo's shares in my portfolio for years now, tactically trimming or adding exposure based on its valuations. As the stock has fallen from its 52-week high of $183/share to the current $144, the environment is ripe for me to provide an update to my last coverage from the end of 2023.

Since my last coverage, the stock has underperformed the broader market (SP500), as income-oriented investors fled towards less risky, yet attractive, fixed-income and concerns grew over the long-term impact of weight-loss drugs from Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lily (LLY), raising worries about demand impact.

Likewise, from a health standpoint, both beverage giants PepsiCo and Coca-Cola (KO) are facing scrutiny from the government due to their potential health implications and market practices, such as price discrimination.

I call this the perfect storm for the company and a potential opportunity for dividend growth investors and investors looking for safety, as PepsiCo's shares are trading at a rare discount.

PepsiCo's Challenges

If you were to choose between owning Coca-Cola's business or PepsiCo's, which one would you choose?

No doubt KO is the undisputed leader in the beverage industry, but PepsiCo's portfolio offers diversified revenue streams from beverages, snack, and convenience foods brands, better withstanding market shocks and product-specific challenges while offering more stable growth.

My pick is PepsiCo.

PepsiCo's EPS growth over the past 15 years has been relatively shallow, 5.7% annually, as a result of a poor operational performance between 2012-2016, with management turning the ship around in 2021-2023, improving its bottom line with growth reaching 8-13% EPS annually.

In 2024, the EPS grew by 7%, helped by markets like Latin America and Europe, with weakness in the US with inflation-wary customers.

Yes, 7% growth is well below the 12% growth achieved the year prior, but 2024 was a year full of challenges with stretched customers and the safety recall in Quaker Foods unit.

On the last earnings call, management guided for relatively mild growth in 2025, expecting "mid-single-digit" EPS growth, similar to analyst's expectations of ~4.7% growth.

Relatively weak growth in 2025 is a direct result of ongoing price increases tailored to offset the declining volume in North America in snacks and beverages, as weaker consumer spending is impacting the company's performance.

Raising prices amidst inflation-wary customers might seem like a counterintuitive strategy, but PepsiCo is in a position where it cannot afford to absorb the extra costs as its operating margin contracted, and 2025-2026 might prove to be challenging years, and the stock price action is a direct reflection of that.

However, if you are a long-term-oriented investor, the undervalued stock is a perfect candidate for one's portfolio. That's why I added more shares to my portfolio, around $150/share, and if the stock price drops towards $135-$140, I'll add more as PepsiCo's fundamentals remain safe and sound.

Rocky Outlook

PepsiCo's products are marketed worldwide, yet the business benefits from secular tailwinds in beverage subcategories like energy drinks and further expansion in markets such as Africa, Asia, and Latin America, which are still underpenetrated, particularly in its snack business.

The good news is that the appetite for beverages and snacks tends to remain stable during economic cycles. JPMorgan, for instance, has raised the odds of a US recession to 60%, up from 30% at the beginning of the year. If this negative forecast materializes, PepsiCo's business should be more resilient, but I would still expect sales and stock price weakness.

The US-induced trade war is likely to lead to higher costs and disruptions in the supply chains. Due to its size, PepsiCo's end-to-end supply chain could prove more resilient, helping to absorb shocks and allowing more effective cost negotiation for its raw inputs, such as sugar, oil, and seasoning purchases, responsible for a relatively minor margin squeeze despite the inflationary environment.

That's not to say that the industry doesn't have inherent challenges that need to be overcome to continue generating attractive returns.

We see customers shift towards more health-conscious alternatives. As 60% of PepsiCo's revenue is generated in North America via Frito-Lay and Pepsi beverages, with another 40% generated from Europe, Asia-Pacific, the Middle East, and Africa, the company is exposed to various regulations.

For instance, increased taxes on sugary products, high-sodium products, saturated fat, and plastic packages all pose challenges to its volume growth and pricing.

Then, we have the GLP-1 receptor agonist drug class that suppresses appetite, altering consumer behavior and potentially impacting the demand for fast foods, beverages, and snacks.

GLP-1 drugs are responsible for making its users feel fuller, leading to lower calorie intake, and while we don't yet have long-term data available on how consumer preference shifts, the reduced consumption of calorie-dense products could have implications on PepsiCo's core product lines.

Since I named this section "rocky outlook," certainly I should add here the risk of sticky inflation, being the byproduct of import tariffs, further impacting consumer spending, PEP's market share, and squeezing margins.

PepsiCo's risks shouldn't be taken lightly. Yet, the business remains a global powerhouse in beverages and snacks, supported by a well-recognized portfolio of consumer favorite brands with a loyal customer base and a $92B annual revenue run rate, confirming its successful manufacturing/supply chain/marketing execution.

As snacks contribute more to the business than beverages, around 54% of the company's revenue, I want to just highlight that back in 2024, PepsiCo controlled 23% of the market, ahead of competitors, and this is, in particular important, as it allows the company to retain industry-leading pricing power.

Let's not forget that PepsiCo has been a major partner to leading retailers such as Costco (COST) and Walmart (WMT) for decades, creating mutual value by PepsiCo better penetrating the market due to favorable treatment and the retailers gaining cost efficiency, something I call a "win-win relationship."

Shareholder Returns

PepsiCo has paid quarterly dividends since 1965, and it earns the company the revered title of Dividend King for increasing the dividend for over 50 years.

Due to the rare 27% drawback from its all-time high, PEP now offers investors an attractive 3.75% dividend yield.

To put this into perspective, PEP hasn't offered this high of a dividend yield in the last decade; only during the short-lived recession caused by COVID-19 did the yield spike to 3.68%.

I find the $1.355/share dividend very appealing. It's supported by a healthy 65% dividend payout ratio, and in the last five years, the dividend has grown at a 7.25% rate.

In fact, despite the headwinds, PEP announced a 5% dividend increase starting June 2025, sticking to its dividend growth policy.

Analysts are forecasting another increase coming next year, that should bring the total annual dividend to ~$6.04, which would mark a 6% increase.

Whether a 5-6% increase is in store for the next year is difficult to say at this stage, but even as the company navigates the challenges, I expect mid to high-single-digit dividend growth is likely until the end of the decade.

This provides a handsome balance between dividend yield and growth.

PepsiCo is also active when it comes to share buybacks, and its float has fallen by 7.1% in the last decade, with investors benefiting from the simple supply/demand dynamics.

Valuation

Overall, the stock market is trading at a premium to its historical valuations (despite the sell-off), and in pricey markets, bargain-hunting is virtually impossible as the tide lifts all boats.

Nonetheless, PEP's shares appear to be in a downward spiral, falling as much as 27% from its all-time high, currently trading at a Blended P/E of 17.6x.

To be fair, PEP's shares were expensive between 2019-2023, trading above the blue line below in a range of 22-28x its earnings.

From my standpoint, buying shares of slowly growing, defensive businesses at a P/E higher than 20, is a good way to earn sub-par ROR.

PEP's 15-year average valuation has been 22.2x its earnings, which gives us a good idea of the 21% discount in today's share price.

However, as I explained above, PEP's near-term outlook is rocky due to inflation, GLP-1 drugs, government scrutiny, and potential recession, all contributing to the stock price weakness.

I don't expect a reversal to its P/E of 22 in the near term, particularly as the forward growth will be pressured in 2025, as per FactSet:

  • 2025: EPS of $8.26E, YoY growth of 1%
  • 2026: EPS of $8.83E, YoY growth of 7%
  • 2027: EPS of $9.44E, YoY growth of 7%

I see a fair valuation right now, around 19-20x its earnings, with a potential for 12-13% total ROR (incl. dividends) over the following few years.

I find double-digit ROR attractive, with a quality holding like PepsiCo because as we face uncertainty in other areas of the market, drinking sodas and snacking on chips is set to stay with us, no matter the economic cycle.

Investor's Takeaway

All in all, PepsiCo's price weakness is fully justified due to sticky inflation, reduced consumer spending, government scrutiny, GLP-1 drugs, and the risk of recession.

However, an opportunity to buy a quality business like PepsiCo is rare, and I expect the negatives are priced in, and in case of recession, the business will likely offer above-average resilience due to its defensive beverage/snack portfolio.

Trading at a 10-year low of 17.6x its P/E, with a 3.75% dividend yield, I am increasing my exposure to PEP's business, looking to add more shares if the price contracts further towards $140/share.

data.title: Bechtle: From Overvaluation Into Fair Valuation
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

I've been covering European IT businesses for some time. Some with success, such as CapGemini (OTCPK:CGEMY) and SAP (SAP), others not so much, such as Atos SE (OTCPK:AEXAY).

While I am in no hurry to invest in the company that I am going to review for you today, I am saying it's a good company to have on your radar going forward because this company is likely to enjoy an upside on a forward basis. It may not be a growth monster - at least not today - but Bechtle AG (OTCPK:BECTY) has moved from a significant position of overvaluation to one of, at the very least, fair value - and this is something that is worth noting.

In this article, I will take you through the basics of the IT company Bechtle, a €6B revenue business that is as old as I am, founded about 40 years ago in Germany. The company trades natively as a component on the MDAX under the ticker BC8 and is headquartered in Neckarsulm, Germany.

Started by a few former students, a typical "garage" type of business, it has grown impressively to its current operations. Also, it's somewhat different in structure than both CapGemini, SAP, and Atos.

Let's see what we have going on under the hood here.

Bechtle - More of a holding than a very clear business

One of the characteristic parts of Bechtle is the fact that it actually owns shares in over 100 businesses across the world. The holding company, Bechtle, is, therefore, no more than a parent organization providing centralized services to subsidiaries. This includes IR services, corporate comms, HR, risk management, and Corporate IT.

However, its subsidiaries are not by name or, as you can see above, in other ways, separate entities. Instead, all subsidiaries usually start with the name Bechtle. Some of the ones included here are Bechtle Logistik & Service, Bechtle SystemHaus Holding, Bechtle Managed Services, etc.

The company's operations are found primarily in Germany, Switzerland, and Austria, meaning the German-speaking part of Europe, but it also has an international profile to its operations. Bechtle operations are split essentially into two businesses, the IT Systemhaus & managed services, which includes every single service business and specialized competence centers across Europe, and an E-commerce IT service with a portfolio of over 70,000 products sold using both traditional catalogue, something still very much "in" in Germany, as well as telesales and online sales.

See why I categorize the company as "interesting" due to this business and sales mix? I do not think you will find a lot businesses operating in this fashion.

Bechtle is an IT company that, unlike some others in Germany, focuses on what makes US-based IT companies appealing. That is a combination of growing earnings as well as growing dividends. Bechtle's dividend comes to 2.1% at this time, and this follows a massive crash since ZIRP. At its peak, Bechtle traded above €61/share for the native, it's now down to €33/share. The company has a market cap of around €4.2B, and, like many German companies, applies an extremely restrictive or conservative use of debt. Current long-term debt/cap is less than 23%.

The company has recorded growth even in difficult market environments and has averaged a rate of EPS growth above 11% per year over the past 20 years. This is well above the market and company average. Furthermore, the company is not a business that "misses" estimates. It hits them - and usually quite accurately, with either miss or beats over the past 10 years, even with a 10-20% margin of error.

I do not believe it inaccurate, therefore, to call Bechtle safe and capable and not prone to surprises. The share price might be - but this is a very different thing.

This lack of surprise for top and bottom line numbers can be seen in the 2024 report, which we have to look at.

The international segment is growing even more than the home market, considered by the company to be Germany. While the company reduced In EBIT for the year, as well as EBIT margin, this was related to cost increases and inflation, which did not spare Bechtle as a business either - much like the other companies on the market, both European and international. The company saw a rise in Gross Margin for the 2024 year YoY, but this was not enough to compensate against the costs seen here.

The company is practicing active cash management to increase cash flow, which has improved OCF from a low of €117M in 2022 to over €550M in 2024, improving liquidity and credit profile (though I would argue this is not even something the company struggles with).

Due to flat earnings levels, the company keeps its dividend flat as well - you'll want to expect the company to size the dividend according to its earnings, but given company communication, I don't expect easy "cuts" here to the payout, despite temporarily lower earnings. This is confirmed by the company's 2023-2025 trends.

The company is an organization that has been awarded a number of public contracts and work, the latest of which is work for Germany's Federal Ministry of Interior/community, and keeps winning EU OCRE tenders, the latest of which is cloud service and research/education in 13 countries.

What I would focus on are the company's basic financials. For 2024, the company generated €6.3B in revenue, upon which it made 18.1% GM. This is lower than our IT peers, but this is not just a service business. The companies then manage a 5-6% operating margin, again low for peers, but there are reasons for it. Given its low debt, the company's interest expenses are very low, and net income margins are at or close to 4%. The company has no SBC or dilution problems, it also doesn't really do buybacks as such. The company's dividend stands at a payout of less than 50% and is safe. Book value based on equity alone is over €15/share, and the company has a net debt of less than €100M (Source: TIKR.com Paywalled Link, Bechtle).

FCF for the company is excellent and has been growing very nicely.

And the margins here are above 7.3%. The company has no cash issues, with over €640M on hand as of the end of the year, which is well over 10% of its market cap. Shareholder yield is, in my view, a good way to value how the company rewards it shareholders and how it performs. Using the dividends paid and net debt paydowns (subtracting debt issued from debt paid back) and looking at market capitalization, we get a shareholder yield for the company of just south of 3%, which is very solid.

Comparing the company to what I view as its closest two peers yields decent results as well.

Fundamentals for the company are very solid, and I don't see any near-term danger or risk of fundamental deterioration. Forecasts are currently at a growth of 5-6% this year, followed by a reversal to double-digit growth rates in 2026E. Keep in mind that this company doesn't really miss any estimates.

Let me show you the valuation.

Valuation for Bechtle - Potentially attractive after a significant ZIRP-related drawdown.

Bechtle usually trades at 26-27x P/E. There is something to be said for the valuation premium being justified given the company's history, operating and gross margins, as well as the company's exposure to the public markets and governmental contracts in Europe. There's also the company's unfailing forecast accuracy to take into account, all of which are positive factors for the business.

The current valuation is closer to 16-17x P/E, and this is enough to mark it an undervaluation. However, in that 26-27x P/E premium, we also have this period to consider - which I do not believe should be considered "valid" in the longer-term framework/valuation context.

So, while a premium may be justified, I'm not putting it at 26-27x P/E. There's too much of 2019-2021 in that, and I have specifically avoided reinvesting in such companies after they drop based on the assumption they will go back up there.

So we'll take a longer-term stance. The 15-20-year average comes closer to 18-20x, which I view as a better estimate. I do not see Bechtle trading as it did post-GFC - the company didn't have a yield (almost) and is much better now than it was at the time.

I would put the company at a maximum P/E of 20x.

Unfortunately, based on a current growth estimate of 5-6% on average for the next few years, that means that the upside is less than 15% per year, even with the company's dividend included. If we were to include the 0.8% shareholder yield we can attribute to net debt paydowns, it might rise above this, but it's still too much of a return issue (with my requirement being 15% or above) for me to go in here. If the company were to drop below €30/share for the native, the return would be good enough, but here it simply isn't.

Because of this, I have no choice but to take a somber and more careful view of Bechtle in my introductory piece. What I want to see is Bechtle at a price where 15% annualized isn't only "just there" but where there even might be a safety margin for it at 19-20x P/E.

That comes to about €30/share or below. Current street targets for the company take a different view, with only a few targets at or below that target of mine. The low-end analyst target for Bechtle at this time is €29/share, with a high of €51/share, coming to an average of about €41/share Despite this upside, only 5 out of 12 analysts have the company at a "Buy", with 6 at either "Hold" or "Underperform", signalling a conviction problem to me. At €33/share compared to the average, there's more than a 24% upside to be had here - yet not even 50% of analysts consider it a buy.

I will come in at a conservative €30/share PT, meaning buying below or at €30/share for the native ticker. This allows an easy 15% or above at 20x P/E, and even a double-digit return at an 18x P/E, which is very close to where the company is trading today. Given its earnings accuracy, its fundamentals, its yields, and its solid base, I consider Bechtle to be worth your attention here.

Closest peers would be CapGemini and Atos or SAP. CapGemini, a French giant I also cover, trades at a lower P/E and has BBB+, as well as a better yield. While the company's growth is lower, I consider CapGemini the far better choice at this time around on a peer basis, which I will illustrate using this 15x P/E upside for the French giant.

CapGemini is, therefore, my first choice in the European IT sector at this time, owing to better fundamentals and valuation. The company is also 4-5x the size of Bechtle in terms of market cap. It comes with other challenges, but I believe these are secondary to valuation here.

Bechtle's ADR is BECTY, a 0.2x ADR, which means that 5 BECTY equals one native share. My target for BECTY, translated and adjusted for size, is $6.76/share.

Therefore, my thesis for the company is as follows.

Thesis

  • Bechtle is a fundamentally solid IT company with an upside at a conservative valuation. We have an upside; we have a relatively conservative valuation in terms of the 20-year average, but unfortunately, it includes exuberant periods of ZIRP, which we need to discount for.
  • That being said, the company's margin, management, debt fundamentals, and results are all positive - and I expect an annualized rate of EPS growth in excess of 5%. The company has none of the risks, such as excessive debt, that weigh other companies down in the same sector.
  • i give the company a conservative PT of €30/share, at which point I would consider the upside attractive enough to invest in.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €30/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Markets Consolidate, But Nervously So, Remaining One Social Media Post Away From Euphoria Or Doom
data.description:
Overview

The postponement of US reciprocal tariffs, the "clarification" that popular consumer products, including those from China, are not subject to the reciprocal tariffs until the levy on semiconductor chips are decided, and a hint from President Trump himself that he is considering a delay in the auto tariffs that were to be effective on May 3 has steadied investor nerves. The dollar is mostly consolidating within the pre-weekend range, and equities have stabilized. In the currency market, sterling and the Australian dollar have shown some independent strength and have extended their gains. The Canadian dollar is threatening the same. Emerging market currencies are mixed, and no clean regional breakdown is evident.

Most Asia Pacific equity markets are extending yesterday's gain. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is up a little more than 1% after advancing 2.70% yesterday. If sustained, it would be the first back-to-back gains since March 18-19. US index futures are firmer. European benchmark 10-year yields are mostly 2-3 basis points firmer. The 10-year Gilt yield is off slightly more than a single basis points and is the best performer among back bond markets today. The 10-year US Treasury yield is practically flat near 4.38%. Gold is consolidating with a firm bias inside yesterday's range, which was inside Friday's range, when the record was set near $3246. May WTI is quiet, mostly between $61.25 and $62.00 today.

USD: The Dollar Index consolidated yesterday, within the pre-weekend range but it still settled lower for the fifth consecutive session. It is trading quietly in a narrow range (~99.50-100.00) inside yesterday's range. The price action looks like nesting before another leg lower. The US threatens that sectoral tariff on semiconductors and pharmaceuticals (and possibly lumber and diary) will still be forthcoming. Yesterday, President Trump said he was considering a delaying the 25% tariffs on autos and parts that were to go into effect on May 3. That and the postponement of the reciprocal tariffs is understood by many as revealing the administration's pain threshold in terms of the dramatic pressure on US stocks and bonds. We suspect that the main check that will arise on US trade policy will not come from the markets or the judiciary, but from Congress, which has the constitutional power to "regulate commerce." It may take a while longer, but the wheels appear to be in motion. Today's April NY Fed manufacturing survey and import/export prices are not typically market-movers. The source of US inflation is not coming from imports. The year-over-year pace may have slowed to 1.4% from 2.0% in March, which would unwind the recent gains. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for unchanged imports on the month.

Euro: The euro stalled yesterday after rising by 3.7% in the previous two session. It is trading inside yesterday's range, which was inside last Friday's. Provided it holds above the $1.1250 area, it looks constructive. A break would initially target the $1.1180 area. Industrial production rose by 1.1% in February % after rising by 0.6% in January (revised from 0.8%). It was well above expectations for a 0.3% rise and matches the largest increase since February 2023. Meanwhile, the slide in the DAX and the US tariffs may have weighed on investor sentiment. April's ZEW survey saw not only the first decline in expectations in three months, but the setback unwound the earlier gains. It fell to -14.0 from 51.6, its lowest since July 2023. The current assessment improved for the fourth consecutive month, and at -81.2 is the best since last August.

CNY: The dollar rose against the offshore yuan for the first time in four sessions yesterday, and simply recouped its pre-weekend loss. It is a bit firmer today, but it remains below last Friday's high (~CNH7.3365). It may continue to consolidate. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rare at CNY7.2096 (CNY7.2110 yesterday) n onshore yuan is practically flat against the dollar this year. That means that it has depreciated against the most of its trading partners. It does not need to offset the US tariff hike with more currency depreciation. How can it? Imagine you are in Beijing. You are watching the US hike tariffs. While the wider change in US foreign policy being implemented by Washington is more disruptive of its alliance, but on trade, China is a key target. Beijing has de-risked from the US. In 2017, the US accounted for about 20% of China's exports. Now around 15%, but that was still $400 bln in 2024. After seemingly announcing prohibitively high tariffs on Chinese goods on April 2, the US indicated that almost 1/4 of its imports from China (consumer electronics) would not face such a tariff after all, and indeed levies collected will returned. This will give producers and importers more time to build inventory before the semiconductor sectoral tariffs are implemented. Beijing's mantra has shifted from "reform and opening up" to "self-reliance and hard work." China orders a halt in Boeing (BA) deliveries today. Estimates suggest Chinese airlines were to take delivery of 10 Boeing 737 Max aircraft. There may be some exceptions granted on a case-by-case basis, depending on what "paperwork and payments" may have been completed before the PRC tariffs were implemented on April 12. Tomorrow, China reports its first estimate of Q1 25 GDP and March details. Beijing says the economy will grow around 5% this year. So, it will.

JPY: Yesterday, the dollar spent the session inside last Friday's range (~JPY142.05-JPY144.65). It is inside yesterday's range today and trading between about JPY142.70 and JPY143.60. The US 10-year yield fell around 11 bps and settled below the pre-weekend low (4.38%). If the nearly 75 bps (from low to high) last week rise in US 10-year yield was exaggerated and extends its pullback, the dollar looks vulnerable. A break of JPY142 could open the door toward JPY140.00-JPY140.50. After a sharp 3.5% drop in core machine orders, a snapback is expected to be the first thing tomorrow. However, the key take-away is that the Japanese economy is slowing considerably this quarter. At an annualized rate, the world's third-largest economy grew an average almost 1.2% in 2024 and that is with a 2.1% contraction in Q1 24. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey puts Q1 25 GDP at 0.3% (annualized) and 0.5% for the next couple of quarters. The swaps market almost 15 bps of tightening discounted, down from 30 bps at the end of March.

GBP: Sterling rose a cent yesterday and tested the $1.32-area, holding slightly below the April 3 high (~$1.3205). It has reached a new high near $1.3240 today, leaving intraday momentum indicators stretched ahead of the North American open. Some attributed it to some short covering against the euro ahead of today's jobs report. Sterling settled above its upper Bollinger Band, which comes in near $1.3200 today. Neither the employment report, nor tomorrow's inflation data, will likely alter views that most likely (90%+ in the swaps market) that the Bank of England will cut rates at its May 8 meeting. All else being equal, the rise of sterling and the decline in oil prices give it scope to reduce rates and support the economy, which practically stagnated in H2 24. Average earnings growth remains more elevated than MPC members prefer even though they softened slightly, and that underscores the gradual pace of the easing cycle. Unemployment remains at 4.4% where it has been now for four months, which is the highest since Q3 21.

CAD: The greenback's session low yesterday was recorded in early European turnover near CAD1.3830. It hardly traded below CAD1.3850 in the local session and rose to session highs around CAD1.3910 after European markets closed and has been capped there today. The US dollar is threatening to extend its slump for a fifth session today. It has not taken out yesterday's low yet, but it looks poised to in the North American session. Canada reports March housing starts and existing home sales, but the focus is on the CPI. A firm reading is expected. The headline rate is expected to rise by around 0.7% which, given the base effect, may mean that the year-over-year rate only ticks up to 2.7% from 2.6%. The underlying core measures are also expected to rise slightly (3.0% from 2.9%). A 0.7% increase in March would put the Q1 annualized pace at 5.7%, but this is partly a payback from the flat reading in Q4. That means that the six-month annualized rate would be around 3.8%, still elevated. The Bank of Canada meets tomorrow, but with next week's election and the firmer core inflation may encourage the standpat stance. It frontloaded cuts last year, and the swaps market has 47 bps of cuts discounted for the remainder of the year, down from about 55 bps at the end of March.

AUD: In the past four sessions, the Australian dollar has rallied nearly 7.25%. It has recovered from a five-year low (~$0.5915) to about $0.6345 yesterday. It is extending its recovery today and approached $0.6380. With one exception, it has not settled above $0.6400 since last December. Momentum indicators are favorable, and Australia made it to US Treasury Secretary Bessent's trade-negotiations short-list, which is also constructive. The futures market has a roughly 30% chance of a half-point cut next month. It was around 65% in the middle of last week. We suspect that the market may still be exaggerating the risk. The minutes from this month's central bank meeting seem to signal a rate cut next month after the election, encouraged by expectations that Q1 CPI (due April 30) will show a moderation in prices, but there did not appear to be a hint that a larger "catch-up" move was being considered.

MXN: After stalling last Thursday and Friday, the dollar took another leg down against the peso yesterday, falling to almost MXN20.02. Recall that last week, the greenback set a two-month high near MXN21.08. It is trapped near yesterday's lows in consolidative turnover (~MXN20.05-MXN20 .11). A break of the MXN20.00 area could spur a re-test of the five-month low recorded on April 3, slightly below MXN19.84. It also held then above the 200-day moving average, which was around MXN19.81. Now it is closer to MXN19.89.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Berkeley: A Luxurious Project Investors Are Betting On
data.description:
Trading at less than 10 times earnings, Berkeley (OTCPK:BKGFY) stock may seem like a no-brainer buy, especially when it's the cheapest UK housebuilder stock amongst the big five. But for all that, a declining earnings outlook over the medium term indicates this could very well be a value trap. That being said, if investors believe in its longer-term project, it may be worth an investment.

Moving Out Costs Berkeley

As a whole, Berkeley's interim results weren't too shabby. Completions were up a healthy 17.8% to 2,103 homes － the company's highest rate in H1 since 2017. Unfortunately, though, this was overshadowed by the relatively stark decline in Average Selling Price [ASP] of 3.8% to £600k. As a consequence, revenue increased by a more modest 7.3% to £1.28B.

Nonetheless, thanks to better economies of scale which were complemented by stabilising build cost inflation, gross margin saw a tick up of 33bps to c.26.5%. This meant that gross profit also rose 8.6% to £339MM. Paired with operating efficiencies in the back office, EBIT margin also realised a lift of 74bps to c.20.2%, resulting in EBIT rising 11.4% to £258MM.

Net finance income was £5MM better than last year, at £10MM. But due to St Edward's shift to lower-value developments in the South East from London this year, JV income went from £54MM to a meagre £7MM this year. Thus, PAT ended up being 7.5% lower at £196MM. This translated to basic EPS dropping 5.8% to 186.8p, as the fall was offset slightly by share buybacks.

Still, management reiterated its guidance over the medium term. It continues to expect PBT of £975MM for FY25 (£525MM) and FY26 (£450MM), with an EBIT margin of 17.5-19.5%. However, it did shift expectations for net cash to a range of £250-400MM by FY26 from a previous £400MM, as they anticipate more cash being used to buy more land, as part of the Berkeley 2035 strategy.

On that point, it's Berkeley's 2035 strategy that is one of the reasons behind why both the team and analysts alike are projecting for Berkeley's top and bottom lines to fall through to FY27. It comes down to two standout reasons － a slowdown in prevailing sales rates, and higher investments in land to scale its Build to Rent [BTR] platform.

Top and Bottom Lines Head South

This is why although peers like Bellway (OTCPK:BLWYF) and Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODF), have seen their order books and sales rates pick up, Berkeley's hasn't. This is because Berkeley's product mix is centred around upscale properties, especially in flats in London and the South-East, where housing starts have come in significantly below targets, and even more so over the past year.

In fact, house prices in the UK has been a story of two tales over the past year. Data from Rightmove (OTCPK:RTMVF) shows quite a disparity in house price appreciation between the North and South － an indicator of supply/demand dynamics. Most prominently, areas like London and the South, where Berkeley builds most of its properties, have experienced much lower increases.

Not only that, it's also worth noting that the Surrey-based builder predominantly builds flats/apartment complexes as opposed to detached or terraced homes. As such, it hasn't been able to realise the appreciation of its properties, as flats have had the lowest price growth over the past year, according to Nationwide data.

Additionally, Berkeley is allocating quite a number of its builds to its BTR portfolio. As a result, the builder will see a reduction in revenue from completion sales, as the revenue is deferred to rental income instead. The idea here is that revenue will accrue over time to 'out-earn' what it would've otherwise gained from just selling the properties themselves.

Moreover, Berkeley can't just build its way to higher revenue either. Given its position in the South-East, where many historical sights are located, getting approval to build new homes is an even longer and more tedious process. The new Labour government's efforts to trim the red tape down are positive, but they may take a while before Berkeley can actualise them into results.

An Expensive Investment?

Having said that, Berkeley still stands to gain over the long term from the Labour government's brownfield-first approach. Officials have proposed changes to the National Planning Policy Framework [NPPF] to prioritise brownfield development, with the policies aiming to transform neglected urban land such as abandoned industrial sites into housing.

This is particularly good for Berkeley because it tends to build many of its apartment complexes on regenerated brownfield land, which is extremely common in London and the South-East. As a matter of fact, c.92.0% of the homes it built in H1 were built on brownfield land. Given the aforementioned regulatory stumbling blocks, these policies are a much-needed bulldozer.

But where long-term investors may find Berkeley's full potential is in its BTR project, with the first deliveries scheduled for FY27. While the board has yet to disclose how much in sales the portfolio could fetch, our calculations indicate anywhere between £80-120MM/yr when all 4,000 units are ready to rent out in FY30, with the scale to build even more thereafter.

Be that as it may, there are also numerous headwinds that could undermine the earnings outlook. Amongst these include the higher Residential Property Developer Tax [RPDT], on top of the 25.0% corporation tax, plus discussions of a Building Safety Levy [BSL], and higher build cost inflation from mandatory wage hikes. These all could constrain Berkeley’s ability to maintain its margins.

But as we don't cover the stock, we refrain from issuing an official rating. That said, our model suggests downside of c.3.0%, with a forward dividend yield of c.6.4%. This is derived by comparing Berkeley's key ratios to their historical and sector(s) averages, before averaging their resulting fair values altogether. Although, we stress that this is contingent on consensus estimates.

Price Model Berkeley Sector AVG 5Y AVG Fair Value
P/B (20% wt) 1.1 1.0 1.6 4,515p
EV/EBITDA (20% wt) 6.4 10.0 8.7 5,489p
P/E (20% wt) 10.2 15.3 11.7 4,972p
FP/E (20% wt) 10.91 16.7 12.8 5,085p
PEG (20% wt) -2.6 1.5 2.1 -2,612p
Fair Value 3,500p

***As a reminder to our readers, Berkeley's primary listing is on the London Stock Exchange (LSE:BKG). Trading this stock on OTCMKTS may present liquidity risks resulting in higher bid-ask spreads. For optimal execution and more accurate price discovery, we recommend assessing the stock on the LSE instead, where possible.

Explaining Our Approach and Model

When it comes to our valuation model, we employ a combination of multiples approach, rather than the infamous Discounted Cash Flow [DCF] model, which many investment banks utilise. To explain, our methodology centres on utilising multiple valuation ratios:

  • P/B (20% wt);
  • EV/EBITDA (20% wt);
  • P/E (20% wt);
  • Forward P/E (20% wt);
  • (Forward) PEG (20% wt);

While we normally prioritise forward earnings metrics because stock prices predominantly reflect future earnings expectations, the lower EPS forecasts have forced us to incorporate some intrinsic value multiples. We've also included trailing ratios in order to provide analytical balance. This is to mitigate the effects of volatility or potential M&A activity.

We believe this approach delivers greater accuracy than a DCF, for the reason that it can account for critical factors such as stock buybacks more accurately. Furthermore, DCFs typically produce significantly higher standard deviations, as minor adjustments to the Weighted Average Cost of Capital [WACC] and/or discount rates can dramatically alter price targets.

In addition to that, DCF models often contain "black box" elements with inadequate explanations regarding the discount rate, WACC determinations, and more crucially, terminal value calculations from perpetual growth rates. On the other hand, our combination of multiples model is more transparent, and more digestible for investors.

Multiples Appendix

  1. P/B (20% wt)
    • As of H1'25 results.
    • Book value is calculated from total equity, divided by basic shares outstanding as of latest earnings RNS.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
  2. EV/EBITDA (20% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1'25 results.
    • Market cap data for EV is calculated from multiplying shares outstanding as of latest RNS with the latest share price.
    • EBITDA is calculated from: EBIT + Depreciation.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes positive EBITDA firms.
  3. P/E (20% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1'25 results.
    • EPS is calculated from profit after tax, divided by basic shares outstanding as of latest earnings RNS.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes money-making firms.
  4. FP/E (20% wt)
    • Usually calculated from using weighted forward projections if current period falls between fiscal years.
    • For example, if the current TTM is to H1'25, the calculation will be: (FY25 projected EPS - H1'25 EPS) + (FY26 projected EPS / 100 x historical weight of H1 EPS on FY EPS).
    • EPS is calculated from FTM profit after tax, divided by projected basic shares outstanding.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by S&P Global Market Intelligence.
  5. FPEG (20% wt)
    • First, CAGR is calculated as: (FY27 EPS / TTM EPS) ^ (1 / 2.5 yrs) - 1.
    • Then, FPEG is calculated as: (Current share price / TTM EPS) / CAGR.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pampa Energia: Strategic Infrastructure With Latent Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Pampa Energy (NYSE:PAM) is an integrated energy company with an exceptional position in Argentina. As the main private electricity generator in the country and the 3rd largest producer of unconventional gas, the company demonstrated consistent results over the past few years, and its 2024 results validate this: during that year, the company achieved record gas production, a 19% increase in adjusted EBITDA, a net profit that doubled in one year, and an EBITDA margin of 50%. This performance is due to a growth in volumes, better prices and cost control, even in the face of a volatile macroeconomic environment.

The company's position is very promising and is reinforced by a robust investment portfolio. The development of the Rincón de Aranda shale oil block in Vaca Muerta, with an expected production of 20,000 bpd by Q2 2025, and its participation in Argentina's first LNG export project afford Pampa access to dollarized revenues.

Likewise, I believe the company's valuation is very attractive: despite the 57% increase in shares over the last year, Pampa is still trading at important discounts: EV/EBITDA (fwd) of 4.68x and P/E (fwd) of 8.57x, compared to the industry medians of 11.15x and 17.6x. These ratios are below Pampa's average of the last 5 years, which seems to indicate that the market has still fully incorporated its performance and projections.

With estimated profits of 135% in 2025, a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 0.6x and an ROE above 20%, Pampa Energía is emerging as a stock with high revaluation potential. In this context, I consider that Pampa represents an excellent buying opportunity for long-term investors looking for value in the Latin American energy sector.

Overview

Pampa Energía S.A. operates in electricity generation, gas and oil exploration and production, and gas transportation through Transportadora de Gas del Sur (TGS), Pluspetrol Partners is the main-integrated energy company of Victoria's private capital in Argentina. For the 7th year in a row, in 2024 the firm was reconfirmed as the largest private energy producer in the country, with 15.3% involvement in national electricity generation and a growth of 4% YoY.

Pampa attained a historical output level in 2024 in the gas sector, with an increase of 21% YoY and a total growth of 80% since 2017. With a growing significant part in domestic supply and export potential, this makes it the third-largest producer of unconventional gas in the country.

The company is, by 2025, carrying out an investment program of almost $700 MM aimed mostly at the development of the Rincón de Aranda block in Vaca Muerta, where it projects production of 20,000 barrels per day by the second quarter of the year. New well boring and construction of support hydrocarbon extraction and filtering facilities are part of the project.

Pampa is also participating in the program, planned to start in 2027, to ship liquefied natural gas (LNG) led by Pan American Energy and Golar LNG. It will hold a 20% stake in the Southern Energy business and supply 22.2% of the natural gas required off its Neuquén Basin properties. With approximately $2.9B in financing, the initiative lets Argentina first access the world LNG market.

Alongside this, Pampa released plans for a new 300 MW wind power project in Bahía Blanca, with the first stage (94.5 MW) to be operational in 2025. With total investment surpassing $500 MM, it emphasizes its status as a major player in the Argentine energy transformation and broadens its portfolio toward clean and sustainable sources.

Although evidence of transformation is present, these projects are undertaken under a difficult macroeconomic environment. With the slow removal of subsidies, the opening of tariffs and the unification of the exchange rate, the new Argentine government in 2025 drives a macroeconomic normalization process. As part of this process, the progressive lifting of capital controls ("cepo cambiario") opens the door to more predictable currency flows and investment planning for exporters and dollarized sectors.

This new setting allows for more private exports, advances regulatory stability, and boosts the profitability of integrated energy businesses. Thanks to size, diversification, and proven execution, Pampa is located among the big possible winners of this new economic and energy cycle.

4Q 2024 and FY 2024

According to data from Pampa Energía, consolidated revenues reached $1.87B in FY 2024, an 8% YoY increase, explained by higher gas volumes for thermal generation, improved local prices, and the incorporation of the PEPE 6 wind farm. In Q4, sales grew 20% YoY, reaching USD 435 MM.

Adjusted EBITDA for 2024 totaled $937 MM (+19% YoY), with a 50% margin. In Q4 2024, this increase was 60% YoY to $182 MM, reflecting an operational improvement in all segments, but especially in electricity generation and the recovery of regulatory remunerations.

Net income doubled YoY, reaching $619 MM (+103% YoY), while the loss from Q4 2023 was reversed in the quarter with a gain of $106 MM. For the year, EBIT was $440 MM (+4% YoY) and in the quarter it grew more than four times, going from $10 MM in Q4 2023 to $48 MM.

By segment, for FY 2024, power generation was the largest contributor to adjusted EBITDA, with $390 MM (+3% YoY), followed by the oil and gas business ($346 MM, +2% YoY).

The petrochemical segment suffered a sharp decline due to lower profitability, while the holding and other segments recorded strong growth, mainly due to extraordinary results and the appreciation of stakes in Transener and TGS.

In operational terms, hydrocarbon production averaged 61.6 thousand barrels equivalent per day in 4Q24 (+9% YoY). Meanwhile, the lifting cost per barrel equivalent was $8.7, increasing due to the development of Rincón de Aranda. In generation, Pampa shipped 4,797 GWh (–4% YoY), with a 94% improvement in availability and unit gross margin ($22.8/MWh, +9%).

In addition, gross financial debt reached $2.079 B, but net debt was reduced to $410 MM, with a robust cash position and financial placements of more than $1.668 B. This led to the company ending FY 2024 with a net debt/adjusted EBITDA ratio of 0.6x, the lowest since 2016.

Valuation

Pampa Energía offers a unique investment opportunity due to the magnitude of the gap between its valuation and its operational strength. Despite a stock market rally of +57% in the last 12 months, its multiples continue to show deep discounts, both with respect to the sector average and its own history. P/E FWD is at 8.57x against the sector's 17.6x (-51%), while the EV/EBITDA FWD is at 4.68x against 11.15x (-58%). Both ratios are also lower than their five-year averages (5.92x and 6.09x, respectively), indicating that not even their own turnaround was yet correctly valued by the market.

Other ratios that reinforce the asset's undervaluation are: EV/EBIT FWD of 6.87x (vs. 17.53x), EV/Sales of 2.33x (vs. 4.20x), PEG ratio of 0.80x (vs. 2.64x) and Price to Cash Flow FWD of 4.12x (vs. 7.63x and 8.65x historic).

The widespread compression of these indicators contrasts with a scenario of strong expansion of results: by 2025, EPS growth of +135.5%, revenues +60.6%, EBITDA +20.4%, EBIT +89.6% and operating cash flow +64.3% are expected. This divergence reveals a typical repricing opportunity: high growth not yet reflected in price.

The company combines this operating momentum with outstanding financial health: its net debt is equivalent to only 0.2x EBITDA, thanks to a cash position of $1.67B and total debt of $2.1B, which positions it among the least leveraged utilities in the emerging world. In addition, its net margin of 32.6% triples the sector average (12.7%) and its ROE reaches the current 21.2%, with estimates that project it to be close to 81% in 2025, leveraged by improving profitability and capital efficiency.

Even under moderate assumptions – such as an EV/EBITDA of 6.5x or a P/E of 11x, still below the global average – the upside potential ranges between 35% and 60%, without the need for radical regulatory changes. This asymmetry between strong fundamentals and depressed multiples positions Pampa as one of the most attractive bets in the Latin American energy segment.

Risks

One of the main risks that the company faces is regulatory risk. Although there are signs of normalization in rates during 2025, this scheme is very uncertain, since it is strongly “tied” to political decisions, which could affect income, mainly from the generation and transportation segments.

Additionally, it should be taken into account that Pampa is exposed to Argentine macroeconomic volatility. Exchange volatility, restrictions in the currency market and high inflation, even in a scenario of sharp decline, continue to be factors to take into account, due to the impact on costs and growth expectations.

Along these lines, exchange rate risk is very significant due to the currency mismatch in which the company operates: while a large portion of its revenues are in pesos, its investments and a significant portion of its debt are in dollars. These currency restrictions may affect foreign payments and financial flexibility.

Likewise, there are environmental risks related to operations in unconventional fields. These projects face increased regulation, which could negatively impact costs or lead to new restrictions. Finally, there is execution risk in Pampa's expansion projects, which could affect expected returns due to cost changes or delays.

Conclusions

Pampa Energía is one of those stocks that the market hasn't fully discovered yet. It has everything a long-term investor is looking for: solid results, visible growth in Vaca Muerta, concrete projects in renewables and LNG, and a healthy balance sheet. Despite that, it continues to trade at very low multiples. From the outside, it may look like "just another Argentine stock," but if you look at the numbers, it's clear that it's far below its value.

With increasingly dollarized revenues, low net debt, and a return on equity that beats the sector average by a mile, Pampa doesn't need a miracle to rise: it only needs the market to start recognizing what's already happening. If you're looking for a real-world play in emerging energy, for me, this is a buy that makes a lot of sense.

data.title: Liberty Global: Making Sense Of The Valuation
data.description:
On the paper, Liberty Global's (NASDAQ:LBTYA), (NASDAQ:LBTYK), (NASDAQ:LBTYB) management presents a $25+ SOTP (Sum-Of-The-Parts) valuation. Reality gives investors a check with a price of $10.5. The market clearly looks at the company through different lenses.

There is the SOTP lens of $25, where Liberty's assets, cash pile, and investments total way above today's share price. Then, there is the free cash flow (FCF) lens, where the market sees the company that just spun off its highest cash-generating asset (Sunrise), leaving it with limited FCF and an unclear strategy for near-term shareholder returns.

There are two ways to move the valuation up toward the SOTP:

  1. Aggressive buybacks or monetizations that prove SOTP is being prioritized.

  2. Normalized FCF growth as capex cycles roll off and high-leverage assets generate cash.

So, Liberty’s future valuation depends on either shifting market perception from the FCF lens to the SOTP lens or improvement in FCF. In this article, I'm going to look at the company from both perspectives, assess the position of each Telco's potential FCF, and discuss what needs to happen for the market to switch the valuation from FCF to SOTP.

This article is not an introduction to Liberty Global's complex business. If you are new to the thesis, I suggest you start with my previous Liberty articles.

Liberty's Current Structure

To understand how to value Liberty, we must start with how it sees itself. Liberty describes its business across three platforms.

Liberty Telecom: European telcos in the UK, Netherlands, Belgium, and Ireland

Liberty Growth: Portfolio of media, tech, and infra investments

Liberty Services: Centralized tech/finance platforms with long-term contracts to Liberty's telcos and potential contracts with the companies from their Growth portfolio.

SOTP Valuation vs. Market Price

As Liberty's management presented their SOTP valuation, they arrived at $25/share when assuming a 1x multiple of MC/EBITDA for their Telcos.

Just 1 additional EBITDA multiple more than doubles our stock. - Mike Fries, Earnings Call (Feb 2025)

On paper, this checks out. Cash, Liberty Growth, and proportional telecom EBITDA support such a valuation. However, the market disagrees with valuing Liberty based on SOTP but rather on FCF, which is currently depressed without Sunrise.

To close this gap between these valuations, Liberty needs visible catalysts: spin-offs, IPOs, and especially buybacks. Until then, SOTP is a theoretical upside, not a market reality.

Strategic Plan for 2025: Re-Rating the HoldCo

…spin-offs. Although as many of you know, this decision is dependent on several tax, legal and financial factors. We could also consider tracking stocks, IPOs. And of course, we always remain open to M&A options. — Mike Fries, Earnings Call (Feb 2025)

While not guaranteed, Liberty laid out a strategic roadmap for 2025:

  • Monetize $500M–$750M in non-core assets
  • Buy back up to 10% of shares (I wish there were more. In previous years, Liberty announced increased share buybacks in the middle of the year, so there is still hope for an increase.)
  • Secure recurring fees from OpCos via Liberty Services (More about it later)
  • Continue exploring IPOs, spin-offs, or tracking stocks

Shareholders were already rewarded by the $10 Sunrise (SNRE) spin-off last year, which has proven the concept works well. Tracking stocks, on the other side, deserves a closer look. John Malone (Liberty's spiritual godfather) has used trackers frequently. Historical examples clearly show that trackers usually trade at a discount unless used only as a short-term bridge to a real spin-off. Creating new trackers would likely tend to reinforce the Holdco discount, not resolve it.

The Road to Free Cash Flow Normalization

For the current year, FCF from Telcos is estimated at £350-400M for VMO2, €300M for VodafoneZiggo, and €180-150M negative for Telenet.

The biggest reason the FCF looks weak right now is the capex cycle. With Sunrise gone, Liberty’s other telco assets need to step up.

The telco's markets can also be brutal, with alt-nets changing the comfortable duopoly/oligopoly markets into a furious fight for each client, with FCF margins nearing zero.

UK (VMO2)

50% ownership in Virgin Media O2 is Liberty’s most mature asset. Capex is stable around at ~£2.1B, driven by fiber buildouts via nexfibre.

…we're generating free cash at VMO2 and VodafoneZiggo as we have in the past and as we expect to in the future…” — Mike Fries, Earnings Call (Feb 2025)

This is a reliable, cash-producing engine that can support buybacks, especially as nexfibre penetration grows. It is questionable when the capex goes down, but as I stated in my previous analysis, we could potentially see up to £1B FCF three years out. For that to happen, the market needs to get more rational. If not, even in a lower capex environment, we could still see £300M FCF.

Netherlands (VodafoneZiggo)

VodafoneZiggo continues to be a strong FCF contributor. Capex guidance remains in line, and the 50% joint venture is expected to distribute €300M in 2025 again. I consider it a dependable, low-drama cash generator.

Belgium (Telenet + Wyre)

Here’s where capex bites. 2025 is a peak Capex year for both the ServCo (Telenet) and the fiber NetCo (Wyre). Belgium is temporarily skewing group capex upward. Crucially, Liberty isn’t paying for this expansion:

We also use the bank market to finance large CapEx builds. We just agreed a EUR 500 million stand-alone CapEx facility for Wyre. We expect capital intensity in the Telenet ServCo to decline in 2026 as the major investments in the mobile network will be completed in 2025, while CapEx will also be fully debt financed… meaning no equity requirement from Liberty Global or Telenet.— Charlie Bracken, Earnings Call (Feb 2025)

This delays FCF from Belgium but also protects Liberty shareholders from dilution or downside. Already in 2026 and beyond, this market could go from a drag to a meaningful €200-300M in FCF.

Telco's Sum Of FCF

To sum it up, counting depressed £375M*50% from VMO2, €300M*50% from VodafoneZiggo, €250M from Telenet, negative $200M for central costs, we arrive at $300M FCF. If we see the improvement from VMO2 after the Capex goes down in 2028, the FCF could rise even above $800M.

$300M and $800M are very different sums, but as I say, it's hard to estimate as it's dependent on the markets getting more rational.

With a Market Cap of $3.7B, this gives us a 12x - 4.6x FCF multiple. This isn't crazy cheap, but don’t forget there is still over $3B in Growth portfolio and $2.2B in cash.

Why does SOTP deserve more weight in the valuation?

The simple answer is the genius corporate structure with the debt and now Liberty Services.

The Debt Structure

The debt sits at the operating level, not at the parent. Each asset carries its leverage, and none of it is recourse to Liberty. The debt is huge, but the HoldCo itself is $2.2B net cash.

This structure provides two key advantages:

  1. If a telco underperforms, Liberty’s exposure is capped.
  2. If a telco is monetized, Liberty captures the full equity upside.

Liberty Services: The Silent Value Sucker

In parallel, Liberty quietly reshaped its corporate structure. "Liberty Services" — which includes "Liberty Tech" and "Liberty Blume" now generates ~$600M in recurring revenue, with contracts signed directly with Liberty’s own telcos and, in the future, with companies from their "Growth Portfolio".

This matters. By shifting costs to Liberty Services, the company creates recurring, contractual cash flow at the HoldCo level.

That means that if Liberty spins off any asset, it still retains service revenues through multi-year agreements. This structure essentially sucks cash flow from their assets and helps preserve value at the top, regardless of how the underlying assets evolve with their isolated debt burdens.

Liberty Growth Portfolio

Liberty Growth includes dozens of stakes. But 75% of this portfolio value is concentrated in 7 companies.

My take:

  • Infrastructure holdings like AtlasEdge and EdgeConnex are valuable and potentially monetizable.
  • Media stakes (Formula E, TelevisaUnivision, ITV) are harder to value and could become distractions. I wasn't happy when Liberty increased its stake in Formula E.

Looking at the depressed valuation, it's clear that investors don’t want Liberty using its cash pile to expand Liberty Growth.

Buybacks or another Deal

Buybacks are the most obvious way to close the SOTP gap. And Liberty is trying - $700M in 2024, another 10% of shares targeted in 2025.

I would argue that 10% buybacks didn't improve the valuation in the past and the company should be more aggressive. But, instead of accelerating repurchases, Liberty has continued to increase Capex and considering more money spent on growth. Rumors of acquiring the rest of VodafoneZiggo hit the market. When the news broke, LBTYA dropped 10% in pre-market. It opened flat, but the message was clear: shareholders want monetization, not expansion.

Conclusion

I consider Liberty's structure complex but elegant. A business that captures all potential upside but is isolated from the risk of default on any of its assets.

On SOTP basis, the shares are deeply undervalued. However, the market refuses to assign that value unless it sees more proof of monetization, buybacks, or rising FCF.

That proof is coming — capex cycles are peaking, isolated debt protects the HoldCo and Liberty Services are quietly shifting value upward. Ultimately, the capital allocation will determine whether all of this pays off.

If Liberty pushes harder into buybacks and spins, the stock should re-rate. If it leans deeper into growth, the return is more questionable.

For now, I am taking advantage of the huge valuation asymmetry and assigning a Strong Buy rating.
